body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"task_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.
body.input: Engineer an in-memory batch ingestor that simulates Azure Service Bus semantics for a single topic with three independent subscriptions. Start by defining a deterministic generator that emits synthetic inventory adjustments (create/update/adjust) with fields: messageId (UUID but seeded), partitionKey (SKU), enqueueTime (monotonic), and a payload checksum. Implement at-least-once delivery using duplicate detection keyed by messageId within a sliding time window. Add a simple lock/settle model: when a subscriber pulls a batch, mark messages invisible for a configurable lock duration; acknowledgements settle them, expirations return them to the queue. Route accepted events to three subscribers: (1) metrics (per-SKU counts, moving receive age, and per-minute throughput), (2) reconciliation (idempotent application to produce final per-SKU quantity), and (3) alerts (flag negative inventory, impossible adjustments, or out-of-order timestamps). Each subscription has its own dead-letter queue after N consecutive failures with reason codes captured. Provide controls to vary batch size, lock duration, and dead-letter threshold at runtime and print a run summary: total enqueued, deduped, delivered, settled, redelivered, and dead-lettered; per-subscription lag timeline; and five sample routed records annotated with subscriber outcomes. Include a short documentation digest (2–4 sentences) on ordering scope (partitionKey level), lock duration vs processing time, and why idempotent writes are essential under redelivery. Acceptance: replaying the same seed yields identical end-state and metrics aside from explicit idempotency counters; artificially inflating processing time above lock duration measurably increases redeliveries without corrupting end-state; duplicate messageIds never change the reconciliation state more than once. Keep the interfaces clean—Producer, Broker, Subscriber—so you can swap retry/backoff policies without changing business logic.

body.input: Build a lightweight Event Hubs partitioning model to reason about hot partitions, consumer lag, and checkpointing behavior under restarts. Generate 2,000 synthetic device metrics across ~200 deviceIds with a stable deviceId→partition hash over 8 partitions. Enforce producer ordering per partition and introduce a per-partition append cap to emulate capacity units; when exceeded, drop events and record dropped counts. Simulate two consumer groups (“analytics” and “monitoring”) with configurable parallelism and maintain per-partition offsets and watermarks. Consumers pull in batches, apply simple transformation (e.g., derive moving average), and checkpoint after each batch. Implement restart of the analytics group mid-stream and demonstrate recovery from durable checkpoints with bounded reprocessing. Print a diagnostic report: per-partition message counts, skew (Gini or top-K share), consumer lag time-series, effective throughput per partition, drops, and a restart summary showing last checkpoint vs first post-restart read. Add a concise doc digest contrasting partitions (ordering/parallelism unit) with capacity (throughput units) and explain why checkpoint granularity trades reprocessing for recovery speed. Acceptance: with production ≤ consumption, lag trends toward zero; increasing consumer parallelism reduces lag without violating partition ordering; after restart, duplicates are limited to the last uncommitted batch by design. Keep all randomness seeded for reproducibility and allow toggling partition count to illustrate skew and scaling behavior.

Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. body.input: Design a hierarchical layout planner for Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 that organizes synthetic user activity events into governance-friendly paths while estimating lifecycle costs. Given deterministic events (userId, eventType, ts), map each record to lowercase, key=value path segments such as /zone=raw/date=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH/userBucket=NN/part-XXXX.json where userBucket=hash(userId)%100. Enforce invariants: fixed segment order, bounded directory depth, and strictly lowercase names. Classify data into hot/warm/cold tiers with simple recency rules (≤7 days hot, ≤60 warm, older cold) and produce an estimated monthly cost table using configurable per-GB storage and access assumptions plus read amplification for typical queries (DAU and events-by-type). Simulate a week of data and demonstrate partition pruning: queries constrained to a date filter should only scan matching directories; show the ratio of scanned-to-total files. Output: sample planned paths, tier assignments, projected costs by tier, and a governance note on atomic directory renames for backfills and using ACLs at folder boundaries. Add a validator that rejects illegal paths (wrong case, missing key segment, extra nesting) and proposes corrections. Acceptance: bucket distribution across userBucket is balanced (no bucket exceeds, say, 2× median count); lifecycle moves keep content-addressable checksums stable (report pre/post hashes); partition pruning demonstrably reduces scanned files when filtering by date or hour. Keep the planner deterministic and side-effect free, printing recommendations instead of touching any real storage.

File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. body.input: Specify and validate a minimal scoring API intended to sit behind an API gateway for online inference. Define two endpoints: GET /health (returns build info and a synthetic dependency check) and POST /predict (accepts {requestId, timestamp, features:{...}} with strict types and bounds). Implement layered validation: schema typing, required fields, numeric bounds, categorical whitelist, and payload size limits. Standardize error envelopes with fields {code, field, message, hint} and make all failures deterministic and reproducible. Wrap predict() with latency sampling; send a fixed set of valid and invalid synthetic requests through and compute p50/p95 latency and error rate. Produce a compact test matrix with edge cases (missing features, wrong types, out-of-range, oversized), run it, and print the first failure per case. Output the final schema (as data), validation report, latency percentiles, and a short policy digest on rate limits per API key, header-based JWT checks, and caching rules (cacheable GET only). Acceptance: identical inputs yield identical outputs; invalid requests never reach the scoring core; and two consecutive runs with the same seed produce stable latency percentiles within small tolerance. Add a small step-load experiment (10→25→50 rps synthetic) to illustrate p95 growth while p50 remains flatter, and recommend a p95 SLO grounded in the results. Version the contract and include deprecation guidance with a target removal date for old fields.

body.input: Model a Key Vault–style configuration facade that enables safe, observable secret rotation without exposing raw values. Accept application configs that reference logical secret URIs like secret://payments-db/connStr?v=1. Provide a pluggable provider (simulated, deterministic) that resolves logical URIs to concrete values, caching entries with per-secret TTLs. Implement version-aware reads: during rotation windows, attempt v2 first then fallback to v1 while emitting structured events recording which version served each call. Add a linter that scans raw configs for high-entropy literals, forbidden substrings (e.g., “password=”), and potential environment-variable leaks; rank by severity and generate remediation hints. Simulate a rotation: stage v2 for two secrets, send read traffic with varying TTLs, complete cutover, then retire v1; verify zero “missing secret” incidents. Outputs: effective config (placeholders only), rotation timeline with served-version percentages, cache hit/miss metrics, and lint findings by severity. Provide an operational note explaining soft-delete and purge protection and why recovery windows matter. Acceptance: rotation never alters functional outcomes for callers; cache respects TTLs and picks up new versions within bounded time; linter flags embedded literals with minimal false positives. Design clean interfaces so future providers (HSM-backed keys, certificates) can be added without changing callers.

This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, body.input: Implement a Cosmos DB–like change feed simulator to explore idempotent downstream processing and continuation tokens. Start from seeded base documents (id, partitionKey, etag, payload) and generate a time-ordered stream of insert/update/delete operations with logical sequence numbers (LSNs) per partition plus a global timestamp. Emit continuation tokens encoding (partition, LSN) positions; allow consumers to start from beginning, now, or a saved token. Enforce partition-key stability and produce tombstones on deletes that remain visible for a retention window. Build a consumer that applies operations idempotently to an in-memory sink, keeps per-document version histories, and persists the last continuation token. Provide an RU charge proxy: charge per operation by type/size, aggregate per partition, and report hot-partition penalties. Outputs: feed slices by partition, consumer checkpoints, final sink snapshot, RU proxy summaries, and a short operator note on backpressure when consumers lag (smaller batches reduce tail latency but increase RU/record). Acceptance: replaying the exact stream yields identical end state; out-of-order reads across partitions still maintain per-partition ordering; and expired tombstones disappear beyond retention. Keep seeds fixed for repeatable evaluation and expose a knob to vary batch size to observe throughput/latency tradeoffs.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. body.input: Draft a compact design for an Azure AI Search–style index over synthetic product documents and run a tiny relevance experiment. Define a schema with a stable key, filterable fields (brand, createdDate), sortable fields (price, rating), and searchable text (name, description). Choose analyzers/tokenizers for English text and add a small synonym map (e.g., “tv, television; lappy, laptop”). Implement a naive TF–IDF scorer with optional synonym expansion and compute NDCG@5 for three representative queries under two configurations: baseline and synonym-augmented. Add faceting on brand and priceBucket, and demonstrate facet counts for “4k tv.” Validate that sortable and filterable fields are not full-text analyzed to preserve exact matching and sorting correctness. Outputs: index schema (as data), top-5 results with scores for three queries, NDCG deltas between configurations, example facet payloads, and a short design note on key immutability, incremental updates vs merges, and analyzer consistency between indexing and search. Acceptance: synonym expansion improves at least one query’s ranking without materially degrading others; faceting and filters behave deterministically; paging uses a stable order via tiebreakers. Keep randomness seeded to allow side-by-side comparisons when toggling synonyms or changing analyzer settings.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"task_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.
body.input: Assemble a retry/backoff policy model for a queue processor balancing throughput, fairness, and error containment. Generate a deterministic mixture of jobs (light/medium/heavy) with class-specific service times and transient error probabilities. Guard concurrency with a token-bucket cap and per-class weights to prevent starvation. On transient failures, apply exponential backoff with full jitter; on hard validation errors, dead-letter immediately with a reason code. Track visibility timeouts to emulate leases; jobs that overrun leases redeliver after timeout unless acknowledged. Produce dashboards as printed summaries: throughput per class, p95 latency, average attempts to success, retry histograms, and dead-letter reasons. Provide a fairness view: admitted share vs offered load and effective share under backpressure. Outputs: per-class metrics, tail latency evolution under increasing offered load, and a concise operator note on sizing visibility timeouts relative to p95 service time and why jitter reduces synchronized retry storms. Acceptance: with fixed seed, results are reproducible; increasing concurrency improves throughput until queueing elevates tail latency; lowering the dead-letter threshold reduces time-to-recovery for poison messages without harming genuine transient recoveries. Keep knobs (concurrency, weights, backoff) adjustable at runtime to observe tradeoffs.

body.input: Create a small data-quality gate for synthetic financial transactions that blocks bad data before analytics. Define a strict schema (id, ts, currencyCode, amount ≥ 0, channel) and implement rule families: schema/type validation, required field completeness, domain checks (ISO currency subset), referential sanity (id uniqueness within a sliding window), and lightweight consistency (non-negative daily sum, no future timestamps). Assign severities (BLOCK/WARN/INFO) and compute a final decision (PASS/WARN/BLOCK). Emit per-rule violation counts and top-N offending records with human-readable reasons and remediation hints. Add a compact column profile (completeness, uniqueness, min/max, top-K categories) to ground the decision. Outputs: gate decision, violation ledger, and column profiles, plus a brief integration hint on exporting counts as custom metrics for dashboards and alerts. Acceptance: replaying the same seed yields identical decisions; tightening domain lists predictably increases violations; injecting an out-of-range amount flips the decision from PASS to BLOCK. Keep the gate composable so new checks (regex on reference keys, cross-record balance assertions) can be toggled without restructuring the pipeline; ensure performance is deterministic by fixing batch sizes and seeds.

You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. body.input: Engineer a point-in-time feature retrieval helper that prevents temporal leakage for downstream scoring. From deterministic synthetic transactions (userId, ts, amount, channel), compute features at a caller-supplied cutoff time: rolling 1/7/30-day spend sums and counts, average ticket, distinct channels in 7 days, and recency of last transaction. Enforce window boundaries that exclude events strictly after cutoff. Provide an interface that atomically returns features and a provenance note indicating the latest event included. Implement a tiny logistic scorer with fixed coefficients to yield a probability per (userId, cutoff). Demonstrate ten example predictions and show leakage-proof behavior by comparing features at T1<T2: cumulative features should not include events after T1. Outputs: feature definitions with window math, sample feature rows, predicted probabilities, and a “leakage proof” table illustrating monotonic relationships. Include a brief ownership/SLA note: freshness target (e.g., up to T−5 minutes), availability, and compatibility guarantees when adding a column. Acceptance: features computed at earlier cutoffs never include late events; predictions are deterministic across runs; requesting a cutoff earlier than the first event yields well-defined zeros/null-safe defaults without exceptions. Seed all randomness and keep computation purely in-memory to ensure repeatable results without external dependencies.

File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. body.input: Prototype a circuit-breaker and bulkhead pattern for downstream dependencies typical of API Management backends. Create synthetic downstreams: payments (fast, occasional spikes), catalog (steady), and profile (slow tail). For each, track rolling error/latency windows and trip a circuit when consecutive failures or p95 latency exceed thresholds; implement half-open probing with limited concurrency before closing. Add bulkheads by limiting concurrent calls per dependency and queueing above limits with bounded wait; requests exceeding wait time fail fast with a clear reason. Build a small routing layer that fans out to dependencies per request and aggregates responses; on circuit open, return partial results with degraded-mode annotations. Outputs: time series of circuit states, rejected vs admitted counts per bulkhead, end-to-end p50/p95, and a degraded-mode sample response. Include a brief operations digest on tuning thresholds and the risks of synchronized reopen storms without jitter. Acceptance: with seeded spikes, circuits trip and recover as designed; bulkheads isolate slow profile from starving payments and catalog; degraded responses are explicit and consistent. Keep behavior deterministic and configurable at runtime to explore sensitivity.

body.input: Simulate Azure Functions–style orchestrations (Durable Functions concepts) using in-memory activities and a replayable event history. Model an orchestrator that coordinates three activities: validate, enrich, and persist for synthetic orders. Record a deterministic event history (scheduled, started, completed) and ensure orchestrator code is replay-safe (side-effect free during rehydration). Implement retry policies per activity with exponential backoff and demonstrate compensating actions for failures after persist (e.g., emit a synthetic cancellation event) while maintaining idempotency keys. Provide a deterministic timer event to model waiting for external signals and a timeout path that posts a “pending review” outcome. Outputs: compact visualized histories for three orders (success, retried success, compensated failure), orchestration latency stats, and idempotency validation (replaying history yields identical state). Include a small doc digest on why orchestrator code must be deterministic and how event-sourced state enables reliable recovery. Acceptance: replays never duplicate side effects; retries follow policy; compensation occurs exactly once; timers awaken the orchestrator deterministically. No real cloud calls—just faithful control-flow semantics.

This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, body.input: Design a minimal Azure Monitor–style metric store with near-real-time ingestion, downsampling, and alert evaluation. Generate deterministic metrics (CPU, memory, errorRate) for five synthetic services. Ingest high-resolution points (e.g., 10-second) into an in-memory store, enforce a retention policy, and produce rollups (1-minute, 5-minute) via fixed aggregators (avg, p95, max). Implement three alert rules: (1) static threshold, (2) rate-of-change, and (3) rolling percentile breach, each with evaluation periods and cooldowns. Add a simple alert state machine (OK→ALERTING→ACKED→OK) and produce notifications with context (current value, threshold, contributing series). Outputs: raw and rolled-up metric counts, latency from ingestion to evaluation, alert timelines, and example notifications. Provide a doc digest discussing the tradeoffs between push vs pull evaluation, downsampling bias, and choosing stable aggregation windows. Acceptance: alerts fire deterministically under seeded spikes; cooldowns prevent alert flapping; rollups approximate raw signals while improving evaluation efficiency. Expose knobs for retention and aggregator choice to observe alert sensitivity and cost implications.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn.
